Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum 4113.jpg
Notes:
From reign of Constantine VII and Romanus II.

About the first, in Enc. Britt.: born September 905, Constantinople [now Istanbul, Tur.] died Nov. 9, 959. Also called Constantine Vii Flavius Porphyrogenitus Byzantine emperor from 913 to 959. His writings are one of the best sources of information on the Byzantine Empire and neighbouring areas. His De administrando imperio treated the Slavic and Turkic peoples, and the De ceremoniis aulae Byzantinae, his longest book, described the elaborate ceremonies that made the Byzantine emperors priestly symbols of the state.

About Romanus: Byzantine emperor from 959 to 963. The son of Constantine VII Porphyrogenitus, Romanus was a politically incapable ruler who left affairs of state to the eunuch Joseph Bringas and military affairs to Nicephorus Phocas; Nicephorus became emperor after Romanus' death with the help of Romanus' widow, Theophano.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum 5989.jpg
Notes:
This is the head of a king's statue, from the Tell Açana (Alalah), from its temple. 19th century BC, Assyrian. Diorite, 19 cm. height.

Tell Açana is the modern name of the ancient site of Alalakh, which was a late Bronze Age city-state. It was founded by the Amorites around 2000 BC, when the first palace was built. In the 18th-16 th century BC, the city was part of the kingdom of Yamhad (modern Aleppo) and governed by Yarim-Lim and his descendents, according to the short chronology found at Mari, at that time Alalakh was destroyed, most likely by Hittite king Hattusili I, around 1580 BC.

In the 15th century BC Alalakh was again the seat of a local dynasty, which was vassal of Yamhad. In the mid-14th century BC, the Hittite Suppiluliuma I assumed control of northern Syria, then including Alalakh, that he incorporated into the Hittite Empire. The city was probably destroyed by the Sea People in the 12th century BC and never reoccupied. Excavations revealed palaces, temples, private houses and fortifications.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Amazon hunting in 2019 05.jpg
Notes: Amazons hunting, 5th century, Harbiye. Inv 820. In the center of the mosaic an amazon has just wounded a lion, which can be seen at the horse's feet. A servant with a sunshade protects the huntress from the sun. The right side of the composition is filled with trees and rocks, while at the left another horse's head and an arm are visible. The mosaic was part of a floor of a villa, the one where the Yakso maosaic was found.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Ananeosis mosaic from Daphne in 2019 03.jpg
Notes: This mosaic from the 5th century was found in Daphne (Harbiye) during a rescue excavation by the Hatay Museum in 2005. The allegoric theme of Ananeosis, that is, waking and rebirth, is depicted as a decorated woman in the centre of a square frame. Over her the inscription Ananeosis is visible. She is crowned with a band of precious stones on a golden ribbon. She carries earrings and a pendant on her neck. Her open right hand reaches up, her left hand holds a branch.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Antakya Archaeology Museum Bios and Tryphe mosaic Bios in 2019 03.jpg
Notes:
Bios (inv. 862a) is personified as a man reclining on a soft pillow and holding a goblet in his right hand.

Tryphe (inv. 862b) is personified as a woman leaning like Bios on a soft pillow and holding a goblet in her right hand.

The two form a pair, from Antakya, 3rd century AD.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Antakya Archaeology Museum Boat of Psyches in 2004 03.jpg
Notes:
Boat of the Psyches. From Harbiye, third century AD, inv. 846. Eros is standing on the wings of two Psyches and whipping them on. 
From the Enc. Britt.: Psyche (Greek: "Soul"), in classical mythology, princess of outstanding beauty who aroused Venus' jealousy and Cupid's love. The fullest version of the tale is that told by the Latin author Apuleius in his Metamorphoses (The Golden Ass). In Greek folklore the soul was pictured as a butterfly, which is another meaning of the word psyche.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Antakya Archaeology Museum Comus mosaic in 2019 01.jpg
Notes:
Comus mosaic
This mosaic pavement was accidentally found and damaged by a villager digging a well. It has two panels which could not be fully repaired. One is mainly geometric decorations, the other, larger one, has a winged figure in a long gown, holding a stick and facing a figure to the right which is too damaged to identify. In the background two doors of an entrance are ajar. The visible figure may be Hypnos (sleep) or Comus (Wikipedia: In Greek mythology, Comus (Ancient Greek: Κῶμος) is the god of festivity, revels and nocturnal dalliances. He is a son and a cup-bearer of the god Dionysus. Comus represents anarchy and chaos). Because of the small tesserae used it is dated to the second century AD.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Apollo and Daphne mosaic 5917.jpg
Notes: Apollo and Dapne. From Enc. Britt.: in Greek mythology, the personification of the laurel (Greek daphnē), a tree whose leaves, formed into garlands, were particularly associated with Apollo (q.v.). Traditionally, the special position of the laurel was connected with Apollo's love for Daphne, the beautiful daughter of a river god (probably Ladon) who lived a pastoral existence in either Thessaly, the Peloponnese, or Syria. She rejected every lover, including Apollo. When the god pursued her, Daphne prayed to the Earth or to her father to rescue her, whereupon she was transformed into a laurel. Apollo appropriated the laurel for poets and, in Rome, for triumphs. Daphne was also loved by Leucippus, who was killed because of Apollo's jealousy.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Birth of Venus mosaic Border in 2019 15.jpg
Notes:
This is a large floor mosaic. It is so large that, it being on a floor, I had to correct perspective to a great extent. Although the general impression is precise, the measurements are not: I estimated visually what they would be, and probably made some errors. The borders are somewhat better, because one could walk around them and take pictures from relatively close by.
It was found in a garden during an excavation in 2011. At the outermost border there is a stepped pyramid, and there are various animal figures inside circles made of meandering vines. It the central board the birth of Venus is depicted. She is shown inside an oyster shell, frontally nude. She is surrounded by Eros, freshwater fairies (nereids) and tritons (top part man, rest fish). A smaller board contains geometric ornaments.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Dionysos and Ariadne mosaic All in 2008 02.jpg
Notes:
From Samandağı, second to third century AD, inv. 945. Dionysus and Ariadne, Env. 945/c. From Samandaĝ, 2nd-3rd century AD. In this mosaic the artis has used the façade of a building both as a background and as a frame to divide his picture into three parts. In the central panel Ariadne is sleeping in a sitting position with Dionysus (mainly erased) standing behind and to the left of her. Between them but still further in the background Eros can be seen . In the right panel a satyr, and in the left a maenad. Above the garlanded architrave is a crater (a large vessel) and arranged on either side of the crater, gryphons, bowls and eagles. 
From Enc. Britt.: (Ariadne:) in Greek mythology, daughter of Pasiphae and the Cretan king Minos. She fell in love with the Athenian hero Theseus and, with a thread or glittering jewels, helped him escape the Labyrinth after he slew the Minotaur, a beast half bull and half man that Minos kept in the Labyrinth. Here the legends diverge: she was abandoned by Theseus and hanged herself; Theseus carried her to Naxos and left her there to die or to marry the god Dionysus; or she died in childbirth on Cyprus.This must be the Dionysus variation:

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Dionysos and Ariadne mosaic Top decoration in 2019 08.jpg
Notes:
Dionysus and Ariadne, 2nd – 3rd century AD, from Samandağ. From the Enc. Britt.: in Greek mythology, daughter of Pasiphae and the Cretan king Minos. She fell in love with the Athenian hero Theseus and, with a thread or glittering jewels, helped him escape the Labyrinth after he slew the Minotaur, a beast half bull and half man that Minos kept in the Labyrinth. Here the legends diverge: she was abandoned by Theseus and hanged herself; Theseus carried her to Naxos and left her there to die or to marry the god Dionysus; or she died in childbirth on Cyprus.
Ancient Greek poets and artists liked to portray Ariadne asleep on the shore of Naxos while Dionysus gazes at her with love and admiration. Ariadne's story was later taken up by European artists, writers, and composers, including Richard Strauss in his opera Ariadne auf Naxos (1912; Ariadne on Naxos).

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Early lion sculpture 5996.jpg
Notes:
From Açana, the entrance to a temple (staircase), 13th century BC, Hittite.

Tell Açana is the modern name of the ancient site of Alalakh, which was a late Bronze Age city-state. It was founded by the Amorites around 2000 BC, when the first palace was built. In the 18th-16 th century BC, the city was part of the kingdom of Yamhad (modern Aleppo) and governed by Yarim-Lim and his descendents, according to the short chronology found at Mari, at that time Alalakh was destroyed, most likely by Hittite king Hattusili I, around 1580 BC.

In the 15th century BC Alalakh was again the seat of a local dynasty, which was vassal of Yamhad. In the mid-14th century BC, the Hittite Suppiluliuma I assumed control of northern Syria, then including Alalakh, that he incorporated into the Hittite Empire. The city was probably destroyed by the Sea People in the 12th century BC and never reoccupied. Excavations revealed palaces, temples, private houses and fortifications.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Eros and Psyche mosaic in 2019 12.jpg
Notes:
Sleeping Eros about to be robbed of his quiver by Psyche. From Samandağı, third century AD. Inv. 1021. This mosaic is in the garden.
From the Enc. Britt.: The fullest version of the tale (of Psyche) is that told by the Latin author Apuleius in his Metamorphoses (The Golden Ass). According to Apuleius, the jealous Venus commanded her son Cupid (the god of love) to inspire Psyche with love for the most despicable of men. Instead, Cupid placed Psyche in a remote palace where he could visit her secretly and, by his warning, only in total darkness. One night Psyche lit a lamp and found that the figure at her side was the god of love himself. When a drop of oil from the lamp awakened him, he reproached Psyche and fled. Wandering the earth in search of him, Psyche fell into the hands of Venus, who imposed upon her difficult tasks. Finally, touched by Psyche's repentance, Cupid rescued her, and, at his instigation, Jupiter made her immortal and gave her in marriage to Cupid.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Evil Eye Mosaic in 2019 08.jpg
Notes:
In 1939 in Çekmece near Antakya a villa or house was found, containing several mosaics. On one of them a blue eye, attacked by a bird, dog, trident, sword and scorpion was named “Evil Eye”, the House of the Evil Eye was born. Three mosaics are figurative: the Evil Eye one, the Heracles’s struggle with serpents and the Lucky Hunchback one.

As for the Evil Eye one: a well-hung bald dwarf advances to the left. He holds sticks in his hands, one forked, it may be a talisman to ward off evil. A text above him means “and you too”, a curse meaning “may it happen to you as well”.

In a book I found more extensively: Attacking the evil eye: The eye is pierced by a trident and sword, pecked by a raven, barked at by a dog and attacked by a centipede, scorpion, cat and a snake. A horned dwarf with a gigantic phallus crosses two sticks. Greek annotation "KAI SU" meaning "and you (too)". Roman mosaic from Antiochia, House of the Evil Eye. Hatay Arkeoloji Müzesi, Antakya, Inv.-Nr. 1024

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Four seasons mosaic All or large view in 2008 03.jpg
Notes: From Daphne, Harbiye. Divided in nine scenes by borders of spirals. The background is white in the central scene, but brick in the others. In each corner a person personifies a season, the other figures have been taken from mythology.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Four seasons mosaic Bellerophon and Steneboia in 2008 11.jpg
Notes:
From Daphne, Harbiye. Divided in nine scenes by borders of spirals. The background is white in the central scene, but brick in the others. In each corner a person personifies a season, the other figures have been taken from mythology.
This is a detail of the scene between Summer and Autumn, "Bellerophon and Steneboia". From the Enc. Britt.: Bellerophon, also called Bellerophontes,hero in Greek legend. In the Iliad he was the son of Glaucus, who was the son of Sisyphus of Ephyre (traditionally Corinth). Anteia(or Stheneboea), wife of Proetus, the king of Argos, loved him; when her overtures were rejected, she falsely accused him to her husband.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Four seasons mosaic Hippolutus and Phaedra in 2008 07.jpg
Notes:
From Daphne, Harbiye. Divided in nine scenes by borders of spirals. The background is white in the central scene, but brick in the others. In each corner a person personifies a season, the other figures have been taken from mythology.

This is "Hippolyus and Phaedra" (between Winter and Spring), the man is Hippolytus. Phaedra to the left, with a servant in the middle.

In Hippolytus, a play by Euripides, (428 BC; Greek Hippolytos) Aphrodite, the goddess of love and sexual desire, destroys Hippolytus, a lover of outdoor sports who is repelled by sexual passion and who is instead devoted to the virgin huntress Artemis. Aphrodite makes Phaedra, wife of Theseus, the king of Athens, fall violently in love with her stepson Hippolytus. Phaedra is deeply ashamed of her illicit passion, but when Hippolytus angrily rejects her love she is so mortified by his denunciation that she cannot forbear from falsely accusing him of rape before she kills herself. Her accusation provokes Theseus into pronouncing a curse on his son that eventually leads to Hippolytus' death. But Artemis reveals Hippolytus' innocence before he dies, and the young man is able to forgive his father, thus freeing Theseus from the dreadful stain of bloodguilt.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Gethosyne mosaic in 2019 01.jpg
Notes:
Bust of Gethosyne
This panel is from a Baths D (AD 300-330, Antakya), excavated in 1933, where in Hall 3 important mosaics were discovered. On a border of stepped pyramids the bust of a woman is represented, her face damaged above the chin. She wears an earring, a brown dress, has a long neck, her body slightly turned relative to her face. Two letters enabled researchers to identify her as Gethosyne, who symbolizes joy and cheer.

Notes:

Tell Tayinat (Tell Tainat) is located about 25km east to the city of Antakya. The site is about 800 meters to the site of Tell Açana. Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Sea Thiasos inv. 829 in 2019 02.jpg
Notes:
From a Roman bathouse there is a series of mosaics, inventory numbers 825, 829 and 830. They represent sea-related scenes. They are on one floor in the new museum, but I here sort the ones with number 829.

In the catalogue it is indicated that they are all from the same room in an Antakya bath house, fourth century AD. I used to have the other mosaics from that bathhouse in a set with these, but decided to order them with the mosaics in general.

From the Wikipedia I understand that a group of followers of a god, often in their ecstacy, was called a thiasos. Most often we see the one of Dionysus, but this must be one of the sea god Poseidon, depicted as a triumphal wedding procession with Amphitrite, attended by figures such as sea nymphs and hippocamps.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Stela of Arsuz 1 5802 (cropped).jpg
Notes:
ARSUZ 1 has a large rosette and a winged sun disk on top. Below these, the Storm God is depicted on a palmette, facing right, and the king is depicted behind him, holding his left wrist. The king probably has a palmette leaf in his left hand and a leather bag or bottle in his right hand, which he is gripping tightly around his neck. The god and the king are wearing the same type of short-skirted garment and boots with turned-up toes. 
An interesting difference in ARSUZ 2 from ARSUZ 1 is that both the king and the god have bare feet. The inscriptions of the steles are copies of each other, except for some spelling differences. The author of the text introduces himself as "King Manana's son, King of Walistin, the hero Suppiluliuma. With the help of the grain god and the wine god, and mentions the prosperity of his time. He then speaks of his victories over enemy countries; he tells how he defeated the city of (A) tana (today's Adana) and the country of (A) hiyawa (Cilicia), and boasts about it. He says that he erected the stele of this powerful Storm God. The last part contains a curse formula against those who would harm the stele. The kingdom of Walistin was located in the Amik plain, and its capital was Tell Tayinat (ancient Kinulua). The name of the country is sometimes Patin, sometimes Ungi in Assyrian sources. The steles can probably be dated to the end of the 10th century - the beginning of the 9th century. The same subject is treated with some differences in the reliefs and inscriptions. For this reason, it can be thought that the steles were erected in different corners of a building.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Thetis and Oceanus mosaic Animals in 2008 09.jpg
Notes:
Thetis and Oceanus mosaic. Antakya, 2nd century AD. Mosaic of the Kalender with Oceanus and Thetis. Enc. Britt. About Thetis: From the Enc. Britt.: in Greek mythology, a Nereid loved by Zeus and Poseidon. When Themis (goddess of Justice), however, revealed that Thetis was destined to bear a son who would be mightier than his father, the two gods gave her to Peleus, king of the Myrmidons of Thessaly. Thetis, unwilling to wed a mortal, resisted Peleus' advances by changing herself into various shapes. But, assisted by the wise centaur Chiron, Peleus finally captured her. 
All the gods brought gifts to their wedding. The child of their union was the warrior Achilles, but, according to some authorities, Thetis bore seven children, all of whom perished either when she attempted to render them immortal by fire or when she destroyed them as the tokens of an unwilling alliance. She befriended Zeus when the other gods revolted, and she rescued both Hephaestus and Dionysus from the sea. She had a sanctuary at Sparta.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Thetis and Oceanus mosaic Animals in 2011 12.jpg
Notes: Thetis and Oceanus mosaic. Antakya, 2nd century AD. Mosaic of the Kalender with Oceanus and Thetis.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Thetis and Oceanus mosaic Oceanus in 2019 04.jpg
Notes:
This is Oceanus. From a mosaic called Oceanous and Thetis. Inv. 850 in a larger setting that ius called the Calendar House. From Enc. Britt.: In Hesiod's Theogony, Oceanus was the son of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaea (Earth), the husband of the Titan Tethys, and father of 3,000 stream spirits and 3,000 ocean nymphs. In Homer's works he was the origin of the gods. As a common noun the word received almost the modern sense of ocean. 
The mosaic is from the second century AD, from a Roman bath house.

Title: Antakya Archaeological Museum Yakto mosaic All 2008 6588.jpg
Notes:
From Yakto, Harbiye, 5th century AD. A woman’s bust is depicted in the central medallion. From the word by her head we know she is the personification of the great spirit, Megalopsychia, She is wearing a white tunic and has a crown on her head, a flower in het right hand and a vase full of flowers in her left hand. Ranged around the medallion are mythological hunters whose names are written beside them. 
The border of the mosaic shows topographical details of life in ancient Antioch.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Achilles mosaic sept 2019 6194.jpg
Notes: Second-third century AD, Antakya. The part shown is the upper right part of a larger panel. It’s the only surviving figure in a larger border, his name is written above him. In missing parts a long spear in his right hand and a shield in the left can be supposed. He is probably moving forward. The usual scene depicted would be Achilles being recognized by Odysseus while hiding amongst the maidens in the palace of Lycomedes, king of Skyros.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Ananeosis mosaic sept 2019 6027.jpg
Notes: From Antakya, 5th century. Within the medallion Ananeosis is personified as a young woman. Byzantine influence is dominant in the working of the face. The medallion is framed with fruit, leaves and bust, and between this frame and the edge of the mosaic there is geometric decoration.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Antakya Sarcophagus 2008 5874.jpg
Notes: Long side 2. Between the columns of a central triangular-roofed aedicula and two vault-roofed aediculas on the side, five figures are shown. A horseman at the centre attacks a lion. One of his two assistants holds the horse’s halter with his left hand, and a baton (lagobolon) with the other. The other assistant holds a horn and a lance. One the corners Zeus’s sons, the Dioskuri, stand. They are favoured figures on sarcophagi, tasked with lessening the pain of death. A dog completes the composition.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Antakya Sarcophagus 2008 5879.jpg
Notes: Long side 2. Between the columns of a central triangular-roofed aedicula and two vault-roofed aediculas on the side, five figures are shown. A horseman at the centre attacks a lion. One of his two assistants holds the horse’s halter with his left hand, and a baton (lagobolon) with the other. The other assistant holds a horn and a lance. One the corners Zeus’s sons, the Dioskuri, stand. They are favoured figures on sarcophagi, tasked with lessening the pain of death. A dog completes the composition.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Antakya Sarcophagus 2008 5880b.jpg
Notes: Rear narrow side. Within a barrel-vaulted aedicula a young girl stands between two males with their shawls around the shoulders. She covers her head with a himation as she is mourning.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Antakya Sarcophagus 2008 5881.jpg
Notes: I have no description of this, the long side 1. As for the lid, part of which we see, I have: A lying couple is a classic of the Sidemara type. Their heads would not be sculpted as part of the whole, but added separately. A woman’s head was found near the sarcophagus. On the lid's front are Eros figures, they are a favourite decoration on lids and sarcophagi, often with animals accompanying them and symbolising union, reproduction, life and joy.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Chresis mosaic sept 2019 6172.jpg
Notes: 1=From the House of Triumph of Dionysos, Harbiye (Daphne). This mosaic was uncovered in room 1 of the house. Within the frame there are two female figures. The one on the left standing, the one to the right sitting on a luxurious armchair with red upholstery, only her lower part is visible, her head is gone. She stretches her right arm to hand a bowl to the standing woman. The latter holds a tray with jewellery, a text near her reads as Chresis, meaning “wealth acquired later” (symbolized by what’s on the tray). A cornucopia with fruits held by the seated woman can be seen as “real wealth” as given by nature. The scene would indicate how natural wealth and generosity trump material wealth, acquired later.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Constantine I period mosaic sept 2019 6105.jpg
Notes: Constantine I period mosaic. From a house in Harbiye (Daphne), first half of 4th century AD. So named and dated because a coin of Emperor Constantine the Great (reigned 30-7-337) was there discovered. Other mosaics in the same house were shipped to museums in Franc, England and the US. Remaining parts from room 4 were assembled in the Antakya museum. Human masks in the corners symbolize the four seasons. A child is picking grapes, a bird perches on a vine. The main panel depicts a scene in a palaestra. Suntanned naked athletes are judged by white-draped referees. All miss large parts of their bodies. To the right there is a scene of an athlete (we see only his feet) is raising his opponent in the air. Further right an athlete is about to blow a tuba, as a sign of being winner. Nice as the description may be, only a fraction of what must have been have been a great scene is left.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Emperor Lucius Verus sept 2019 6081.jpg
Notes:
Wikipedia: "Lucius Verus (/ˈljuːʃəs ˈvɪərəs/; Latin: Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus; 15 December 130 – 23 January 169 AD) was the co-emperor of Rome with his adoptive brother Marcus Aurelius from 161 until his own death in 169. He was a member of the Nerva-Antonine dynasty. Verus' succession together with Marcus Aurelius marked the first time that the Roman Empire was ruled by multiple emperors, an increasingly common occurrence in the later history of the Empire.

The eldest son of Lucius Aelius Caesar, first adopted son and heir to Hadrian, Verus was born and educated in Rome where he held several political offices prior to taking the throne. After his biological father’s death in 138, he was adopted by Antoninus Pius, who was himself adopted by Hadrian. Hadrian died later that year, and Antoninus Pius succeeded to the throne.

Antoninus Pius ruled until 161 and was succeeded by Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius. "

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Emperor Macrinus bust sept 2019 6077.jpg
Notes:
Wikipedia: [Macrinus ] was Roman Emperor from April 217 to 8 June 218. He reigned jointly with his young son Diadumenianus. Macrinus was by origin a Berber from Mauretania Caesariensis. A member of the equestrian class, he became the first emperor who did not hail from the senatorial class and was the first emperor from Mauretania. Before becoming emperor, Macrinus served under Emperor Caracalla as a praetorian prefect and dealt with Rome's civil affairs. He later conspired against Caracalla and had him murdered in a bid to protect his own life, succeeding him as emperor.
He was executed in Capadoccia, after Heliogabalus took over as emperor. After Macrinus' death, the Senate declared him and his son enemies of Rome and had their names struck from the records and their images destroyed. So an image of him must be rare.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Kerdon house mosaic sept 2019 6156.jpg
Notes: This mosaic panel was uncovered in the course of a rescue excavation at Maşuklu Neighbourhood of Antakya. It originally covered a room of 34 sq.m. The structure surviving at foundation level is located within the territory of ancient Antiocheia and it is thought to have been destroyed already in antiquity. The area outside the border is 60 cm wide and plain white in colour. Next is a band of 6 cm width. Between this band and the main central panel is a wide border of 1.90 m width decorated with black and deep red coloured rosettes. The figure is placed within a square of 1 m. The name of the male figure from Antiocheia is inscribed in the upper left part reading ΚΕΡΔWN (KERDON); he holds a palm branch in his left hand and a bell in his right. The figure's body is adorned with laurel wreaths worn diagonally across and he is crowned with a laurel wreath. The style of placing the figure within a small area framed with a wide border is known from the Early Roman Imperial period mosaics uncovered at Antiocheia and Daphne (Harbiye). This mosaic panel displays similitude with other mosaics uncovered in the region and attributed to the second-third centuries AD. Coins uncovered are also attributed to the latter half of the second, and first half of the third century AD, thus confirming this dating.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Narcissus and Echo Mosaic All in 2019 0002.jpg
Notes:
This is Narcissus and Echo. Harbiye, third century AD, inv. 938. The figure on the left is Echo, Narcissus looks at his reflection, Eros has a torch in his hand, leaning towards the water.
From the Enc. Britt.: Echo (was) a mountain nymph, or oread. Ovid's Metamorphoses relates that Echo offended the goddess Hera by keeping her in conversation, thus preventing her from spying on one of Zeus' amours. To punish Echo, Hera deprived her of speech, except for the ability to repeat the last words of another. Echo's hopeless love for Narcissus, who fell in love with his own image, made her fade away until all that was left of her was her voice.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Oceanus and Tethys mosaic in 2004 10.jpg
Notes: A mosaic called Oceanous and Thetis, inv. 1013. From Enc. Britt.: In Hesiod's Theogony, Oceanus was the son of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaea (Earth), the husband of the Titan Tethys, and father of 3,000 stream spirits and 3,000 ocean nymphs. Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Ram heads mosaic sept 2019 5955.jpg
Notes: End 4th century, early 5th . A mosaic from a heavily damaged room in a house, found when a villager dug a well. Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Skeleton mosaic sept 2019 5915.jpg
Notes: In a floor of a triclinium one of the panels is that of a skeleton, with a text “Euphrosynos”: “enjoy, have fun, cheer up”. A notice explains that in the 1st century BC skeletons started to be used. This mosaic is from the 3rd or 4th century when scenes of bathing and banquets (convivium) represent the most important activities of Roman socio-cultural life.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Skeleton mosaic sept 2019 5990.jpg
Notes:
To be precise: only the left panel is a skeleton picture.

In a floor of a triclinium one of the panels is that of a skeleton, with a text “Euphrosynos”: “enjoy, have fun, cheer up”. A notice explains that in the 1st century BC skeletons started to be used. This mosaic is from the 3rd or 4th century when scenes of bathing and banquets (convivium) represent the most important activities of Roman socio-cultural life. I also show a picture of a photo showing the excavations.

A panel next to the skeleton shows what is explained as a man pointing at a sundial, with a word near him "Trechedipnos" or "run for dinner", further to his right a man has "Akairos" (Ill timed), so the scene shows a latecomer or a parasite, for a dinner.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Soteria mosaic sept 2019 5958b.jpg
Notes: This mosaic was found in the large square frigidarium of the Apolausis Baths, a small but well-preserved baths a Narlıca, some 9 kilometres from Antakya, in the 1930’s. The female figure constituting the main theme is named Soteria (literally: saviour). She is surrounded by a circular band and is depicted as a female bust rendered over the waistline. Her slightly turned head is crowned with a gold wreath of laurel leaves, which join at a green gem over her forehead. Her hair falls down her shoulders. She wears a shawl over a white dress, covering her left shoulder and breast. She has several pieces of jewellery. That a bejewelled figure representing the saviour is depicted in a bathhouse may be explained with her being a symbol believed to protect health. inv. 977

Notes: From Daphne, Harbiye, 3rd century AD. Man and woman are shown reclining on a couch. In the centre there is a round table with appetizers on it, and a wine jug. A servant stand waiting on the right. A harpist plays on the left.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Thalassa and the nude fisherman mosaic sept 2019 6169.jpg
Notes: Scene from mosaic from Yakto, Harbiye, 5th century AD. The naked Thalassa is rising from the waves with an oar in her right hand and a dolphin in her left. A serpent encircles her body and there are lobster claws in her abundant hair. Young boys fishing or riding on dolphins make up the rest of the mosaic. Inv 1017

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Theatrics mosaic sept 2019 6059e.jpg
Notes:
I was informed about an article, "New Menander mosaics from Antioch" in American Journal of Archaeology, 4 October 212, Vol. 116. This enabled me to identify pictures. Here we see the whole set of frames.
First, left, Menander’s Theophoroumene. Next Synaristosai. Then Philadelphoi. And to the right Perikeiromene.

Title: Antakya Archaeology Museum Tryphé mosaic in 2019 02.jpg
Notes:
Personification of Tryphe, Dahne, 4th century AD. Tryphe was the Greek personification of "Good Life" (the museum catalogue that I used calls it "drunkenness")
The Wikipedia states: "Tryphé (Greek: τρυφἠ) -- variously glossed as "softness", "voluptuousness", "magnificence" and "extravagance", none fully adequate—is a concept that drew attention (and severe criticism) in Roman antiquity when it became a significant factor in the reign of the Ptolemaic dynasty."

Title: Antakya Children in 2004 03.jpg
Notes: In 2004 I had a new digital camera, and in Antakya at the time that was a rather flashy object. And not needing to buy expensive film made it possible to take many pictures. In particular children frequently asked to have their picture taken. Often they combined in groups, each kid trying to be as photogenic as possible. This led to a lot of moved images, with a few people in focus, others out of. The better ones I show. Later kids would be surrounded by mobiles, and taking this type of picture became rare.

Title: Antakya Church of St. Peter exterior ın 2025 05.jpg
Notes: The early Gothic facade was erected by the Crusaders (restored in 1863). The interior was transformed into a three aisle church. Some remains of a mosaic floor are fifth century. To the left of the altar is a - now caved-in, tunnel early Christians would use to flee in case the authorities were after them. To the right of the altar is a font with water dripping down the rocks that is supposedly medicinal. It sued to be called for paptisms. A notice inside indicates that Pope Paul VI paid the place a visit and promised a plenary indulgence to whoever came here.

Title: Antakya Habib-i Nejjar Mosque Courtyard in 2004 08.jpg
Notes: This mosque seems to be a former Byzantine church that was chenged into a mosque in the 13th century. The minaret was added in the 17th century.

Title: Antakya Habib-i Nejjar Mosque Courtyard in 2008 03.jpg
Notes: This mosque seems to be a former Byzantine church that was chenged into a mosque in the 13th century. The minaret was added in the 17th century.

Title: Antakya Habib-i Nejjar Mosque Interior general in 1996 01.jpg
Notes: One of pictures taken inside the prayer hall. Note that in 2004 the floor was still covered (like in many mosques in the country, then) with individual carpets. That gave mosques an extra atmosphere. In 2019 the usual industrial carpets had displaced them.

Title: Antakya Mahremiye camii in 2011 03.jpg
Notes: This mosque has entrances to its courtyard at two sides, one at a busy street in the market area. On the courtyard there also is a (wooden) medrese. It provides a pleasant oasis of rest in the busy market area. Its construction date I could not find, on the web I found a document in Tuirkish that states 1720 is the earliest date of mentioning it, but there is no foundation stone.

Title: Antakya Meydan Camii Minaret in 2004 14.jpg
Notes: This mosque has entrances to its courtyard at two sides, one at a busy street in the market area. It provides a pleasant oasis of rest in the busy market area. Its construction date I could not find, on the web I found a document in Turkish that states 1720 is the earliest date of mentioning it, but there is no foundation stone. The mosque has a wood and glass exterior that was later taken off.

Title: Antakya Museum Hotel Geometric mosaic sept 2019 5671.jpg
Notes:
One of some pictures of, mainly geometric, mosaics that adorn large floor areas. Also shown the connecting structures, and the space in general. Some remains of buildings are in the area too. 
The region often suffers from earthquakes, as a result some of the floors have been distorted, showing wave patterns. In general the patters remain clearly visible.

The region often suffers from earthquakes, as a result some of the floors have been distorted, showing wave patterns. In general the patters remain clearly visible.

Title: Antakya Museum Hotel Helicon mosaic sept 2019 5668.jpg
Notes:
I think the name to the right can be read as elikoon, and with the help of the Wikipedia I think that may be Helicon. A reason is the related myth: In Greek mythology, two springs sacred to the Muses were located here: the Aganippe and the Hippocrene, both of which bear "horse" (ἵππος híppos) in their names. In a related myth, the Hippocrene spring was created when the winged horse Pegasus aimed his hoof at a rock, striking it with such force that the spring burst from the spot.

And as Pegasus figures in the main mosaic, I think I’m right. I’d apreciate help to identify the figure to the left.

2nd century mosaic.

Title: Antakya Museum Hotel Pegasus mosaic sept 2019 5644 ne.jpg
Notes:
One of many pictures of a large mosaic, called the Pegasus Mosaic (because the winged horse figures prominently). I used one picture in the series several times, as it shows a lot. I moved it, turned it around, made several crops and show a colour-corrected version. Pegasus is in the centre.

Individually taken pictures are also in this series.

2nd century mosaic.

One of many pictures of a large mosaic, called the Pegasus Mosaic (because the winged horse figures prominently). I used one picture in the series several times, as it shows a lot. I moved it, turned it around, made several crops and show a colour-corrected version. Pegasus is in the centre.

Individually taken pictures are also in this series.

2nd century mosaic.

Title: Antakya Ulu Cami Courtyard in 2004 01.jpg
Notes: On the web I found: Grand Mosque. It is thought that the mosque was built in 1271 by the Sultan of the Mameluke Baybars. It has a rectangular plan extending in the east-west direction. The courtyard, which is entered through the gates in east and west direction, has a large, stone pavement and a fountain. The high minaret of the mosque, which was built in Ottoman style, was repaired several times. In the construction of mosque and madrasa rooms, smooth cut stone was used.

Title: Antakya Çarşı areas uncovered parts New buildings in 2008 28.jpg
Notes: I am not quite sure if this can still be called Çarşı area. I found some activities had moved several hundreds of meters or more, but soon the old atmosphere appeared (except when all was closed). One of pictures taken during visits over many years to the markets area. Some parts were covered later, and maybe older pictures will be in the “uncovered” category. Some are not of the best quality, but as so much was destroyed I think a bad picture is better than none at all. Pictures of specific subjects will be in sub-categories of the “Çarşı areas” category.

Title: Destruction in Turkish city of Antakya ESA24720887.gif
Notes:
This animation shows the destruction of the Turkish city of Antakya after the devastating earthquake that struck Türkiye and Syria on 6 February. The first image, captured by Copernicus Sentinel-2, show the city of Antakya before the earthquake on 25 January 2023, while the second image shows the city on 14 February 2023.
In response to the earthquake, the International Charter ‘Space and Major Disasters’ was activated. By combining Earth observation assets from different space agencies, the Charter provides satellite images of the affected areas to define the extent of the disaster and support local teams with their rescue efforts.
Read the full article: Satellites support impact assessment after Türkiye–Syria earthquakes

Title: Hatay (tr).svg
Notes: Hatay (tr)

Title: Hatay - ethnolinguistic map.svg
Notes:
Late 20th-early 21st century language distribution. Turkish (red). Arabic speakers (green) are shown by religious affiliation: Alawite (circle), Christian (triangle), Sunni (square), Bedouin Sunni (rectangle), Jewish (rhombus).


In this work, Arnold Werner states scope and methodology of his research and areas covered:

  • Werner, Arnold (1996). "Arabian dialects in the Turkish province of Hatay". Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference of l'Association Internationale pour la Dialectologie Arabe held at Trinity Hall in the University of Cambridge, 10-14 September 1995: 1-10.


Map data is based on the research of Arnold Werner:


In this work, Werner gives a detailed description of all Arabic speaking villages covered in his research:

  • Werner, Arnold (1998) Die arabischen Dialekte Antiochiens, Otto Harrassowitz Verlag, pp. 329−354. ISBN: 9783447040471.


In this work, Werner composed a map showing all Arabic speaking villages in Hatay covered in his research and also of surrounding Turkish villages, of which my map is based upon:

  • Werner, Arnold (2000) "The Arabic dialects in the Turkish province of Hatay and the Aramaic dialects in the Syrian mountains of Qalamun: Two minority languages compared" in Owens, Jonathan , ed. Arabic as a minority language, Walter de Gruyter, p. 368. ISBN: 9783110805451.


In this work, Werner composed a smaller map of the area focusing on the Antakya area in Hatay showing Arabic speaking villages covered in his research:

  • Werner, Arnold (2004). "Homonymenfurcht in den arabischen Dialekten Antiochiens". Jerusalem studies in Arabic and Islam 29: 139.


NOTE: My map does not show all existing Hatay settlements and their linguistic composition. Arnold Werner states that his research only covered half of the Arabic speaking villages (some 50 out of a 100). Turkish speaking villages shown on map are those covered by Werner's research. My map is based on Werner's map and data. All locations on my map replicate the geographical placement of locations, as done by Werner.

At the time of Werner's publication of his works (early 2000s), a few villages had different names from what they are now and he shows them with those place names. On my map the current names are given of those few places. These settlements are: Soğukoluk > Güzelyayla; Yiğityolu > Karbeyaz; Asi > Aknehir; Güneydam > Turfanda (in this case, old name was restored).

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (01).jpg
Notes: The Mosaic Museum in Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (02).jpg
Notes: One of the main halls in the Mosaic Museum at the Hatay Archeology Museum, there is a mosaic Megalopsychia on the floor, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey,

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (03).jpg
Notes: Megalopsychia, a mosaic from Yakto, Harbiye, 5th century AD. The medallion in the middle depicts a female bust. We know from the word at her head that she is the personification of the great spirit, Megalopsychia. She is wearing a tunic and a crown on his head, a flower in his right hand and a vase full of flowers in his left hand. Around the medallion are mythological hunters, whose names are written next to them. The edge of the mosaic shows topographic details of life in the ancient Antioch

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (04).jpg
Notes: Two female figures, the one on the left is standing, the other to the right sitting on a luxurious armchair with red upholstery, only her lower part is visible. She stretches her right arm to the standing woman. She is holding a tray with jewellery, a text near her says "Chresis" (meaning “wealth acquired later"), Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (05).jpg
Notes: The mosaic of Narcissus and Echo, Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (06).jpg
Notes: The upper part of the mosaic is decorated with eagles and griffins. In the middle, Ariadne sits with Eros overhead, and another figure stands beside her. On the right is Dionysus and on the left Maenada (female follower of Dionysus). The lower part is decorated with geometric patterns. Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (07).jpg
Notes: The mosaic called Oceanous and Thetis (from the fourth century AD, was found in Harbiye), Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (08).jpg
Notes: The mosaic called Oceanous and Thetis (from the fourth century AD, was found in Harbiye), Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Hatay Archeology Museum (09).jpg
Notes:
A mosaic of the evil spirit (Kakodaimon) next to an evil eye. The evil eye is surrounded by a raven, a trident, a sword, a scorpion, a snake, a centipede and a dog. It symbolizes misfortune. 
Hatay Archeology Museum, district Antakya, Hatay Province, Turkey

Title: Ibradı Garden District
Collection: Bing and Harrington Balkan Archive, Aga Khan Documentation Center at MIT
Notes: Ibradı, TurkeyIbradı is a small town in the Antalya province of Turkey, inland from the south Anatolian coast on the Mediterranean. The town sits in the rugged Taurus (Toros) mountains above and to the west of Akseki. The region has a long history of earthquake activity. Ibradı has a tight urban center on the southern slope of Kirca Dagi mountain. A garden district lies in the valley south of the center, and contains the best soil for agriculture. This area has been cultivated for an extended period.

Title: Çardak, Dwelling 1, overall view. Akşahap, Turkey
Collection: Bing and Harrington Balkan Archive, Aga Khan Documentation Center at MIT
Notes: Akşahap is a small village in the Antalya province of Turkey on the southern edge of the Taurus mountains. The area was settled during the 4th millenium BCE and was part of the Hittite empire. The open wooden structure, çardak or köşke, is also known locally as an ayazlık. This çardak (ayazlık) faces northeast and serves as an outdoor cover for the ground level as well as a working space for those above. The village is reached from a small road that is an off-shoot of the route between Ibradı and Akseki.

Title: Çukurova.svg
Notes: Çukurova (Cilicia Pedis) imposed on Mersin, Adana, Osmaniye and Hatay provinces. Çukurova image has been derived from File:Turkey topo.jpg, where green (sub 50 m) regions were selected. It is loosely based on this non-free map. The background map was File:Adana in Turkey.svg. Inkscape and GIMP.
